question: Venice is built on 118 small islands, but what unusual material supports the foundations of its buildings?
answer: Wooden piles driven into the mud
choices: Wooden piles driven into the mud
Concrete platforms laid on the seafloor
Stone arches spanning the lagoon
Floating pontoons anchored to bedrock
context: Millions of alder and oak trunks were hammered into the soft lagoon mud beneath Venice. Submerged wood doesn't rot without oxygen, so these piles have stood for over 1,000 years. The city is slowly sinking anyway — partly because 20th-century factories pumped groundwater out from underneath it.

question: The Amazon River discharges so much freshwater into the Atlantic Ocean that you can taste it how far from the shore?
answer: About 100 miles (160 km)
choices: About 100 miles (160 km)
About 10 miles (16 km)
About 500 miles (800 km)
About 1 mile (1.6 km)
context: The Amazon carries about 20% of all the freshwater that rivers deliver to the world's oceans. Its plume of brown, sediment-rich water dilutes the salty Atlantic so dramatically that sailors historically used it to refill their casks. The river's mouth is so wide — around 150 miles across — that no single bridge has ever been built across it.

question: Ireland has no native snakes. According to legend this is Saint Patrick's doing, but what is the real scientific reason?
answer: Ireland became an island before snakes could migrate there after the last ice age
choices: Ireland became an island before snakes could migrate there after the last ice age
The Irish climate is too cold and wet for any snake species to survive
Snakes were hunted to extinction by early Irish settlers thousands of years ago
Ireland's soil chemistry is toxic to reptiles but harmless to mammals
context: After the last ice age ended around 10,000 years ago, rising seas cut Ireland off from Britain before snake populations had time to migrate northward. Britain has three native snake species — they got there just in time. New Zealand, Iceland, and Antarctica also have no native land snakes, for similar reasons of isolation.

question: The Sahara Desert is the world's largest hot desert, but which continent has the largest desert of any kind?
answer: Antarctica
choices: Antarctica
Africa
Asia
Australia
context: A desert is defined by its lack of precipitation, not its temperature. Antarctica receives less than 200mm of precipitation per year across most of its interior — less than the Sahara in some parts — making it the world's largest desert at 14.2 million square kilometres. The Sahara covers about 9.2 million. The coldest, driest, and windiest continent is technically also the largest desert.

question: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level, but which mountain's summit is actually farthest from the centre of the Earth?
answer: Mount Chimborazo in Ecuador
choices: Mount Chimborazo in Ecuador
Mauna Kea in Hawaii
Mont Blanc in France
Denali in Alaska
context: Earth is not a perfect sphere — it bulges at the equator. Because Ecuador sits almost exactly on the equator, Chimborazo (6,263m) is about 6,384 km from Earth's centre, while Everest (8,849m) is only about 6,382 km away. Everest wins on altitude above sea level, but Chimborazo's summit is the point on Earth's surface closest to outer space.

question: Lake Baikal in Russia is the world's deepest lake. If you emptied it, how long would it take all the world's rivers combined to refill it?
answer: About one year
choices: About one year
About one week
About ten years
About one hundred years
context: Lake Baikal holds approximately 23,000 cubic kilometres of water — about 20% of all the liquid freshwater on Earth's surface. It's so deep (1,642 metres at its lowest point) that if you stacked the Eiffel Tower on top of itself nine times, the top would barely reach the surface. The lake is also ancient: at 25–30 million years old, it's the world's oldest lake.

question: New Zealand has almost no native land mammals apart from bats. What filled the ecological roles that mammals play elsewhere?
answer: Birds
choices: Birds
Large reptiles and lizards
Insects and spiders
Freshwater fish
context: New Zealand was isolated for 80 million years before humans arrived. With no land predators, birds evolved to fill roles mammals occupy elsewhere: the kiwi became a ground-dwelling creature that sniffs out worms like a hedgehog, the kakapo became a fat flightless parrot that grazes like a sheep, and the now-extinct moa grew to 3.6 metres tall. When Polynesian settlers arrived around 1280 AD, they brought rats and dogs — and the extinctions began.

question: The Great Barrier Reef is so large it can be seen from space. What is it actually made of?
answer: The skeletons of tiny coral animals
choices: The skeletons of tiny coral animals
A continuous limestone shelf rising from the seafloor
Compressed layers of seaweed and algae
A single species of hard coral forming one connected organism
context: Corals are animals, not plants. Each tiny polyp builds a hard calcium carbonate skeleton around itself. When it dies, its skeleton remains, and new polyps grow on top. The Great Barrier Reef is built from the accumulated skeletons of billions of polyps over hundreds of thousands of years. It stretches 2,300 kilometres — longer than the US west coast — and is visible from the International Space Station.

question: The Mariana Trench is the deepest place on Earth. How does its depth compare to the height of Mount Everest?
answer: The trench is deeper than Everest is tall
choices: The trench is deeper than Everest is tall
They are almost exactly the same depth and height
Everest is about twice as tall as the trench is deep
The trench is about half as deep as Everest is tall
context: The Challenger Deep, the lowest point of the Mariana Trench, reaches about 11,034 metres below sea level. Mount Everest stands 8,849 metres above it. If you placed Everest at the bottom of the trench, its peak would still be over 2 kilometres underwater. The pressure at the bottom is about 1,000 times the pressure at sea level — equivalent to having 50 jumbo jets stacked on top of you.

question: Iceland sits on the boundary between two tectonic plates. What unusual thing can you do at Þingvellir National Park there?
answer: Walk between two continents
choices: Walk between two continents
Watch lava flow directly into the sea
Stand at the exact geographic North Pole
See the sun at midnight from underground
context: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge — the boundary between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates — runs straight through Iceland. At Þingvellir, the rift is wide enough to walk through. The two plates are pulling apart at about 2.5 centimetres per year, the same rate your fingernails grow. Iceland exists only because a volcanic hotspot produces enough lava to keep building the island up as the plates tear it apart.

question: The Dead Sea is so salty that swimmers float effortlessly on its surface. Where is it located?
answer: On the border of Jordan and Israel
choices: On the border of Jordan and Israel
In the interior of Saudi Arabia
On the coast of Egypt
Between Iraq and Iran
context: The Dead Sea is about 9.6 times saltier than the ocean — so dense that your body bobs at the surface with no effort. It sits at 430 metres below sea level, the lowest point on Earth's land surface. It's called 'dead' because almost nothing can survive in water that salty. It's also shrinking: water that once flowed in from the Jordan River is now diverted for agriculture, and the sea loses about a metre of depth every year.

question: Angel Falls in Venezuela is the world's highest uninterrupted waterfall. How tall is it?
answer: About 979 metres — nearly 1 kilometre
choices: About 979 metres — nearly 1 kilometre
About 200 metres — taller than a skyscraper
About 3,000 metres — taller than most mountains
About 100 metres — about the height of Big Ben
context: Angel Falls is so tall that much of the water evaporates or turns to mist before it reaches the bottom. It drops off the edge of the Auyán-tepui plateau — a flat-topped mountain that inspired Arthur Conan Doyle's novel 'The Lost World.' The falls were unknown to the outside world until American aviator Jimmie Angel crash-landed on the plateau in 1937. They're named after him, not the heavenly kind.

question: The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889 for the Paris World's Fair. What was it originally supposed to be?
answer: A temporary structure that would be demolished
choices: A temporary structure that would be demolished
A permanent monument to the French Revolution
A lighthouse to guide ships up the River Seine
A radio transmission tower for the French military
context: Gustave Eiffel's tower was designed as the entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair and was supposed to be torn down after 20 years. It was saved because it turned out to be useful as a radio antenna — the French military used it to intercept enemy communications in World War I. Today it's the most visited paid monument on Earth, with about 7 million visitors a year. Eiffel himself had a private apartment at the top.

question: Russia is so large that when it is morning in the west, what time of day is it on its eastern coast?
answer: The next day has already begun
choices: The next day has already begun
It is still the previous evening
It is noon — exactly 12 hours difference
It is the same time — Russia uses one time zone
context: Russia spans 11 time zones — more than any other country. When it's 9 AM in Kaliningrad (Russia's westernmost territory on the Baltic Sea), it's already 6 PM the same day in Kamchatka, Russia's far east. The country covers about 11% of Earth's land surface and stretches from Norway in the west to within 90 kilometres of Alaska in the east. If Russia were a continent, it would be the largest.

question: The Sahara Desert occasionally gets coated in a layer of reddish dust that blows thousands of miles away. Where does it land?
answer: The Amazon rainforest
choices: The Amazon rainforest
Northern Europe and Scandinavia
The polar ice caps
The bottom of the Atlantic Ocean
context: Every year, roughly 22 million tonnes of Saharan dust crosses the Atlantic and fertilises the Amazon rainforest with phosphorus — a mineral the nutrient-poor Amazon soil desperately needs. The same dust clouds that make Spanish sunsets orange are feeding the world's largest rainforest. Scientists call the two ecosystems 'the lungs and the heart' of the planet, connected by an invisible river of dust 5,000 kilometres wide.

question: Mauna Kea in Hawaii is often called the world's tallest mountain. Why doesn't it beat Everest in the record books?
answer: Most of it is underwater
choices: Most of it is underwater
Its peak is measured from a different baseline
It sank after a volcanic eruption lowered its summit
It was disqualified because Hawaii is an island
context: Mauna Kea rises 10,210 metres from the ocean floor to its summit — nearly 1,400 metres taller than Everest measured the same way. But since 6,000 metres of it sit beneath the Pacific Ocean, only its 4,205-metre peak is above sea level. It's a shield volcano grown over a hotspot in the middle of the Pacific Plate. The summit is so high and dry and dark that it hosts some of the world's best astronomical observatories.

question: The Atacama Desert in South America is one of the driest places on Earth. Some weather stations there have never recorded what?
answer: Any rainfall at all
choices: Any rainfall at all
Any temperature above freezing
Any wind stronger than a breeze
Any cloud cover whatsoever
context: Parts of the Atacama receive less than 1 millimetre of rain per year on average — and some areas haven't seen measurable rain in recorded history. It's dry for two reasons: the Andes mountains to the east block moisture from the Amazon, and the cold Humboldt Current offshore chills the air so it can't hold much moisture. Despite being a desert, the Atacama sits at high altitude — many parts are above 4,000 metres — so it's also bitterly cold at night.

question: The Great Rift Valley stretches through East Africa. Scientists believe it is slowly tearing the continent apart. What will East Africa eventually become?
answer: A new island, separated from the rest of Africa by a new ocean
choices: A new island, separated from the rest of Africa by a new ocean
A mountain range as the land buckles and folds upward
A massive depression below sea level, like a giant valley
A volcanic plateau that merges with the Arabian Peninsula
context: The Somali tectonic plate is pulling away from the Nubian plate at about 7mm per year. In 5–10 million years, the Afar Triangle at the rift's northern end will flood with seawater from the Red Sea, and the Horn of Africa will drift away as a new continent. We can already see the early stage: Ethiopia's Afar region has a valley floor that sits below sea level in places, and the rift's lakes — Tanganyika, Malawi, Turkana — are among the deepest in the world.

question: The Midnight Sun is experienced in the Arctic and Antarctic. What causes the sun to stay above the horizon for 24 hours?
answer: Earth's axial tilt
choices: Earth's axial tilt
The polar regions being closer to the sun in summer
The curvature of the Earth bending sunlight over the horizon
Reflection of sunlight off polar ice keeping the sky bright
context: Earth spins on an axis tilted about 23.5 degrees relative to its orbit around the sun. In summer, the pole tilts toward the sun, so it never rotates into shadow — the sun just circles around the horizon without setting. In winter, the same tilt means it never rises. The Arctic Circle and Antarctic Circle mark the lowest latitudes where this happens at least once a year. In Svalbard, Norway, the sun doesn't set for about four months.

question: The Congo River in Africa is the world's deepest river. How deep does it get?
answer: Over 220 metres deep
choices: Over 220 metres deep
About 30 metres deep
About 80 metres deep
About 500 metres deep
context: The Congo plunges through a narrow canyon called the Malebo Pool and reaches depths of around 220–250 metres — far deeper than any other river on Earth. Most rivers are under 10 metres deep. The Congo is also unusual because it flows both above and below the equator, meaning it always has a wet season happening in part of its watershed — so unlike most rivers, its flow never drops dramatically. It's the second-largest river by discharge, after the Amazon.

question: Madagascar is the world's fourth-largest island. Why does it have so many animals found nowhere else on Earth?
answer: It split off from Africa 165 million years ago and evolved in isolation
choices: It split off from Africa 165 million years ago and evolved in isolation
It was never connected to any other landmass and life arrived by sea
Its unusual climate killed off all species that originally migrated there
Humans introduced animals from around the world which then evolved separately
context: Madagascar broke away from Africa before most modern mammal groups had evolved, then drifted into the Indian Ocean where it sat in isolation for millions of years. The result: 90% of its wildlife exists nowhere else. Lemurs thrive there because the monkeys that wiped them out elsewhere never arrived. The island has five species of baobab tree — Africa has just one. Scientists sometimes call it 'the eighth continent' because its biodiversity is so distinct.

question: Greenland is the world's largest island, but it's covered by an ice sheet up to 3 kilometres thick. What does Greenland look like underneath all that ice?
answer: A bowl-shaped basin, mostly below sea level
choices: A bowl-shaped basin, mostly below sea level
A flat featureless plain at roughly sea level
A mountain range even taller than the Alps
A series of large islands separated by fjords
context: The weight of Greenland's ice sheet has pushed the bedrock down like a mattress under a heavy sleeper. If the ice melted, much of central Greenland would sit below sea level — at least until the land slowly rebounded upward, a process that would take tens of thousands of years. If all of Greenland's ice melted, global sea levels would rise by about 7 metres, enough to flood most of the world's coastal cities.

question: Mount Fuji is Japan's highest mountain and an iconic symbol of the country. What type of mountain is it?
answer: A volcano
choices: A volcano
A glacially carved peak
A fold mountain formed by colliding plates
A dome formed by rising magma that never erupted
context: Mount Fuji last erupted in 1707, covering Tokyo (then called Edo) in ash and creating a new crater on its flank. It's considered dormant rather than extinct — scientists watch it carefully. The mountain's perfect cone shape is typical of stratovolcanoes: it's built from alternating layers of lava and volcanic ash from many eruptions over hundreds of thousands of years. About 300,000 people climb it every year, almost all in July and August.

question: Atolls are ring-shaped coral islands found across the Pacific and Indian Oceans. What lies in the centre of the ring?
answer: A shallow lagoon
choices: A shallow lagoon
A dormant volcano
A freshwater lake fed by underground springs
Solid coral reef above the waterline
context: Charles Darwin correctly figured out how atolls form in 1842 — decades before anyone could verify it by drilling into one. A coral reef grows around a volcanic island. As the volcano slowly sinks (because the ocean crust beneath it cools and subsides), the reef keeps growing upward. Eventually the volcano disappears below the surface, leaving only the ring of reef behind. The lagoon in the centre is the shallow water over where the volcano once stood.

question: The Nile flows northward through Africa to the Mediterranean Sea. Which direction do most of the world's major rivers flow?
answer: Rivers flow in all directions — toward the nearest ocean or sea
choices: Rivers flow in all directions — toward the nearest ocean or sea
Mostly northward, because gravity pulls water toward the equator
Mostly southward, from highland poles toward low tropical coasts
Mostly eastward, following Earth's rotation
context: Rivers flow downhill — that's it. The direction depends entirely on the landscape. The Nile flows north because the highlands of East Africa slope northward toward the Mediterranean. The Amazon flows east to the Atlantic. The Congo flows northwest. The Ob in Siberia flows north. The Mississippi flows south. Gravity doesn't care about compass directions, and Earth's rotation has no meaningful effect on river flow.

question: Canada has the longest coastline of any country. How long is it?
answer: About 202,000 km — long enough to circle Earth five times
choices: About 202,000 km — long enough to circle Earth five times
About 30,000 km — about as long as the USA's coastline
About 500,000 km — long enough to reach the moon
About 12,000 km — similar to Australia's coastline
context: Canada's coastline is so long partly because it's so jagged — thousands of islands, bays, inlets, and fjords in the north multiply the total dramatically. The measurement also depends on scale: a more detailed measurement with a shorter ruler finds more nooks and crannies and gives a longer result. This phenomenon is called the 'coastline paradox,' and it means no two sources quite agree on any country's exact coastline length.

question: Pompeii was buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. Why is the city so remarkably well-preserved?
answer: A thick layer of volcanic ash sealed it airtight
choices: A thick layer of volcanic ash sealed it airtight
It was submerged in lava which hardened into protective rock
Builders used special materials that resist volcanic heat
The city was underground before the eruption buried it further
context: Vesuvius ejected about 4 cubic kilometres of pumice and ash that blanketed Pompeii to a depth of 4–6 metres within hours. The anaerobic (oxygen-free) environment inside this cap preserved wooden furniture, food on tables, graffiti on walls, and even the impressions of people's bodies. Some victims were clutching coins; others were shielding their faces. The city wasn't excavated until the 18th century — nearly 1,700 years after it was buried.

question: The Great Wall of China is often said to be visible from space. Is this actually true?
answer: No — it is too narrow to see with the naked eye from space
choices: No — it is too narrow to see with the naked eye from space
Yes — astronauts regularly photograph it from the International Space Station
Only in winter when snow makes it stand out against dark ground
Only the widest sections near Beijing are visible on a clear day
context: The Great Wall averages about 5–8 metres wide — similar to a highway. From the International Space Station (400 km up), seeing it unaided would be like spotting a human hair from 2 km away. Chinese astronaut Yang Liwei reportedly admitted he couldn't find it during China's first crewed spaceflight in 2003. The myth was even printed in Chinese school textbooks. Highways, airports, and reservoirs are far easier to spot from orbit.

question: Tokyo, Japan is the world's most populous city. How many people live in its greater urban area?
answer: About 37 million
choices: About 37 million
About 12 million
About 60 million
About 20 million
context: The greater Tokyo metropolitan area — including Yokohama, Saitama, and Chiba — holds roughly 37 million people, making it larger than most countries. Yet Tokyo is remarkably safe, clean, and functional. It runs some of the world's most punctual trains (average delay measured in seconds), produces some of the world's best food, and has experienced falling crime rates for decades. The city was largely rebuilt after firebombing in World War II — most of what you see today was built after 1945.

question: The Northern Lights (aurora borealis) produce spectacular colours in the sky. What actually causes them?
answer: Charged particles from the sun colliding with gases in Earth's atmosphere
choices: Charged particles from the sun colliding with gases in Earth's atmosphere
Sunlight reflecting off the polar ice caps at low angles
Moonlight bending through layers of cold Arctic air
Static electricity building up in the upper atmosphere during storms
context: The sun constantly streams particles into space — the solar wind. Earth's magnetic field deflects most of them, but near the poles the field lines dip toward Earth, funnelling particles into the upper atmosphere. When they collide with oxygen and nitrogen atoms, they excite the atoms and release energy as light. Oxygen at higher altitudes produces red, lower down it produces green. Nitrogen produces blue and purple. The dancing motion comes from the constantly shifting solar wind and magnetic field.

question: The Pacific Ocean covers about one third of Earth's entire surface. How big is it compared to all of Earth's land combined?
answer: The Pacific is larger than all land on Earth put together
choices: The Pacific is larger than all land on Earth put together
The Pacific is about the same size as all land on Earth
The Pacific is roughly half the size of all land on Earth combined
The Pacific is larger than Africa and Asia combined but smaller than all land
context: All the continents and islands on Earth add up to about 149 million square kilometres. The Pacific Ocean alone covers about 165 million square kilometres — about 11% larger. From the shore of Peru in South America to the Philippines is about 19,000 kilometres. The Pacific was named by Ferdinand Magellan's crew in 1521 because they found it remarkably calm after rounding the stormy tip of South America. They chose the Latin word for 'peaceful.'

question: Death Valley in California is the hottest place ever recorded on Earth. What temperature was measured there in 1913?
context: Death Valley is so hot partly because it's the lowest place in North America — 86 metres below sea level. Surrounding mountains trap hot air in the valley and block sea breezes. The dark valley floor absorbs heat and radiates it back at ground level, creating a self-amplifying oven effect. Despite this, Death Valley has permanent residents and a national park with over a million visitors a year. The salt flats on the valley floor were once the bottom of a lake.

question: Singapore is one of the most densely populated countries on Earth. What is unusual about its geography?
answer: It is a city-state — the entire country is a single city on a small island
choices: It is a city-state — the entire country is a single city on a small island
It has no freshwater sources and relies entirely on desalinated seawater
It sits on the equator and has no seasons, just permanent summer
Its land is entirely artificial — it was built on reclaimed ocean floor
context: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world (along with Monaco and Vatican City). Its main island covers about 733 square kilometres — roughly 3.5 times the size of Washington D.C. — with about 5.6 million people. It has expanded its land area by about 25% since independence through massive land reclamation projects, importing sand from Indonesia and Malaysia to literally build new land. It's now a global hub for shipping, finance, and air travel.

question: Australia is both the world's smallest continent and the world's largest island. What type of animals are most uniquely associated with it?
answer: Marsupials, which carry their young in pouches
choices: Marsupials, which carry their young in pouches
Reptiles, which make up the majority of Australian land animals
Venomous creatures — Australia has the most venomous species per square kilometre
Birds, which evolved on the continent before mammals arrived
context: Australia separated from other continents before placental mammals (the group that includes dogs, horses, and humans) had evolved and reached it. Marsupials — which give birth to tiny, undeveloped young that continue developing in a pouch — filled almost every ecological niche. Australia has kangaroos as its grazing animals, wombats as its burrowers, Tasmanian devils as its scavengers, and quolls as its small predators. Monotremes — egg-laying mammals like platypuses — are even older and also found mainly in Australia.

question: The Taj Mahal in India is one of the world's most famous buildings. Why was it built?
answer: As a tomb for the emperor's wife who died in childbirth
choices: As a tomb for the emperor's wife who died in childbirth
As a temple dedicated to the goddess of love
As a palace for the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan
As a monument to celebrate India's independence from Persia
context: Shah Jahan, the Mughal emperor, built the Taj Mahal between 1631 and 1648 as a mausoleum for his third wife, Mumtaz Mahal, who died delivering their 14th child. About 20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants spent 22 years building it. The white marble was quarried 320 km away and hauled by elephant. Shah Jahan was later imprisoned by his own son in a nearby fort — from which he could see the Taj Mahal across the river until he died.

question: Hawaii is located in the middle of the Pacific Ocean, far from any tectonic plate boundary. What created it?
answer: A volcanic hotspot — a plume of heat rising through the middle of a tectonic plate
choices: A volcanic hotspot — a plume of heat rising through the middle of a tectonic plate
A crack in the ocean floor where two plates are slowly pulling apart
An ancient meteor impact that caused volcanic activity
A collision zone where the Pacific plate dips under another plate
context: The Hawaiian Islands formed as the Pacific Plate slowly drifted northwest over a fixed hotspot in Earth's mantle. Each island is an older volcano that drifted away from the hotspot and went extinct. The Big Island is directly over the hotspot and is still actively erupting — Kilauea has been erupting almost continuously since 1983. A new underwater volcano, called Lō'ihi, is currently forming about 35 km southeast of the Big Island and will eventually break the surface in about 10,000–100,000 years.

question: The Netherlands has reclaimed large areas of land from the sea. What are these reclaimed areas called?
answer: Polders
choices: Polders
Dykes
Levees
Marches
context: About 27% of the Netherlands sits below sea level — in some places 7 metres below. For over 1,000 years, the Dutch have pumped out the sea using windmills, then diesel pumps, to create flat agricultural land called polders. Without constant pumping, large parts of the country would flood within days. The Dutch are now some of the world's leading experts in water management and sell their flood-control expertise to countries like Bangladesh and the USA.

question: Istanbul, Turkey is the only major city in the world to sit on two continents. Which two?
answer: Europe and Asia
choices: Europe and Asia
Europe and Africa
Asia and Africa
North America and Europe
context: The Bosphorus strait — about 700 metres wide at its narrowest — splits Istanbul between its European and Asian sides. This waterway is also one of the world's busiest shipping lanes, connecting the Black Sea to the Mediterranean. Istanbul has been the capital of three empires: Roman (as Byzantium), Byzantine (as Constantinople), and Ottoman. It was renamed Istanbul in 1930. The city's strategic position — controlling the only sea route between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean — made it one of the most fought-over cities in history.

question: The Galapagos Islands are famous for their unique wildlife. What famous scientist visited them in 1835 and later developed a groundbreaking theory partly based on what he saw?
answer: Charles Darwin
choices: Charles Darwin
Alfred Russel Wallace
Alexander von Humboldt
Louis Agassiz
context: Darwin spent five weeks in the Galapagos in 1835 aboard the HMS Beagle, collecting specimens of finches, tortoises, and mockingbirds. He noticed that different islands had slightly different species — finches with different beak shapes suited to different foods. This observation, combined with years of further study, helped him develop the theory of evolution by natural selection, published in 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859. The islands are so remote that their animals evolved with no natural predators and remain famously unafraid of humans.

question: Antarctica is protected by an international treaty. What activity, which has made some countries very wealthy, is banned there?
answer: Mining
choices: Mining
Scientific research
Tourism
Flying over it by aircraft
context: The 1959 Antarctic Treaty and its subsequent 1991 Protocol on Environmental Protection banned all mining activity in Antarctica for at least 50 years (until 2048, when signatories can request a review). This is significant because Antarctica is believed to hold enormous reserves of coal, iron ore, and potentially oil. It also bans nuclear testing and military activity, making Antarctica the world's only continent that has never had a war fought on it.

question: The Gobi Desert in Central Asia is cold enough to get snow in winter. Why is a cold desert still called a desert?
answer: Deserts are defined by low rainfall, not by temperature
choices: Deserts are defined by low rainfall, not by temperature
The Gobi only gets snow once a decade so it technically qualifies
Desert is another word for barren landscape regardless of climate
Cold areas are counted as deserts if no trees grow there
context: The Gobi receives about 190mm of rain per year — comfortably within the desert definition of under 250mm. Winter temperatures can plunge to -40°C, and summer temperatures can reach 45°C: one of the greatest temperature ranges of any place on Earth. Despite this, the Gobi is home to Bactrian camels, snow leopards, and the Mongolian wild ass. Dinosaur fossils — including the first discovered dinosaur eggs — were found in the Gobi in the 1920s.

question: Niagara Falls straddles the border between the USA and Canada. Roughly how much water flows over it every second?
answer: About 2,400 cubic metres — enough to fill an Olympic swimming pool every second
choices: About 2,400 cubic metres — enough to fill an Olympic swimming pool every second
About 100 cubic metres — roughly filling a large room every second
About 50,000 cubic metres — enough to fill 20 Olympic swimming pools per second
About 500 cubic metres — enough to fill a small apartment building every second
context: Niagara Falls carries more water than any other waterfall in North America. But water volume is diverted into tunnels for hydroelectric generation — especially at night and in winter — so tourists rarely see full flow. Since 1960, flow over the falls has been regulated by treaty between the US and Canada. The falls are slowly eroding upstream at about 30cm per year. At that rate, they will reach Lake Erie and drain it in about 50,000 years.

question: The South Pole sits in the middle of Antarctica on a high plateau. How high above sea level is the ground at the South Pole?
answer: About 2,835 metres — higher than most of the Alps
choices: About 2,835 metres — higher than most of the Alps
At sea level — Antarctica's ice rests on seafloor
About 500 metres — a modest elevation
About 5,000 metres — higher than most Himalayan passes
context: Antarctica is the highest continent on Earth by average elevation — about 2,300 metres. The South Pole sits on top of an ice sheet that is about 2,850 metres thick at that point. Beneath the ice, the actual bedrock is much lower — around 100 metres above sea level. The altitude makes the South Pole even colder than its latitude alone would explain: the US Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station experiences temperatures as low as -83°C.

question: The Amazon rainforest is often called the 'lungs of the Earth.' What gas does it produce in enormous quantities?
answer: Oxygen
choices: Oxygen
Carbon dioxide
Water vapour
Nitrogen
context: The Amazon generates about 20% of the Earth's oxygen through photosynthesis — billions of trees converting carbon dioxide and sunlight into sugar and oxygen. But the 'lungs' metaphor isn't quite right: the Amazon also consumes roughly as much oxygen as it produces through decomposition and respiration. Its more important role is as a carbon store: the trees lock up an estimated 150–200 billion tonnes of carbon. When they burn, that carbon returns to the atmosphere as CO2.

question: The Silk Road connected Europe to Asia for centuries. Despite the name, what was the Silk Road?
answer: A network of trade routes, not a single road
choices: A network of trade routes, not a single road
A paved Roman road running from Rome to Beijing
A river route connecting the Mediterranean to China via India
A road built specifically to transport silk that later opened to other goods
context: The 'Silk Road' is a 19th-century term invented by a German geographer. It was never a single road — it was a shifting, branching network of overland and sea routes connecting the Mediterranean world to Central and East Asia. Silk was one of China's most valuable exports, but the routes also carried spices, glass, horses, paper, printing, religion, and disease (the Black Death likely travelled these routes). The routes were most active from about 130 BCE to 1450 CE.

question: Indonesia is an archipelago — a chain of islands. Roughly how many islands make up the country?
answer: About 17,000 islands
choices: About 17,000 islands
About 300 islands
About 3,000 islands
About 100,000 islands
context: Indonesia's 17,000+ islands span an area wider than the contiguous United States, from Sumatra in the northwest to Papua (New Guinea) in the east. About 6,000 of the islands are inhabited. Four of its islands — Java, Sumatra, Borneo (shared), and Sulawesi — are among the world's largest. Java alone is home to over 140 million people, making it the world's most populous island. Indonesia sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire and has over 130 active volcanoes.

question: The Stonehenge monument in England was built using massive stones weighing up to 25 tonnes. Some of these stones came from Wales, over 240 kilometres away. How did prehistoric people likely transport them?
answer: By boat along the coast and rivers, then on sledges overland
choices: By boat along the coast and rivers, then on sledges overland
By rolling them on logs across the land using ramps
By packing them in ice during winter and sliding them across frozen ground
By dragging them with teams of oxen along cleared forest paths
context: The bluestones at Stonehenge came from the Preseli Hills in Wales. Modern experiments suggest they were loaded onto rafts, moved by sea around the Welsh coast, floated up the River Avon, and then dragged the final 30 km overland. The larger sarsen stones came from Marlborough Downs, about 25 km away. Stonehenge was built and rebuilt in stages from about 3000 to 1500 BCE — work spanning 1,500 years. Why the bluestones were brought from Wales specifically remains genuinely mysterious.

question: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai is the world's tallest building. What time of day can a person on the upper floors watch the sunset after someone on the ground floor has already seen it set?
answer: About three minutes later
choices: About three minutes later
About thirty seconds later
About twenty minutes later
It is visible at the same time regardless of floor
context: At 828 metres, the Burj Khalifa is tall enough that the curvature of Earth makes the sun visible longer from the top. The difference is about 3 minutes. This means that on the floors above the 80th or so, Ramadan technically begins a few minutes later than at ground level — a genuine theological puzzle that Islamic scholars have had to rule on. The building has 163 floors and its own internal bus system. Elevators travel at 10 metres per second.

question: Antarctica is the coldest continent on Earth. What is the lowest temperature ever recorded anywhere on the planet?
answer: -89.2°C (-128.6°F) at Vostok Station, Antarctica
choices: -89.2°C (-128.6°F) at Vostok Station, Antarctica
-62°C (-79.6°F) in Yakutsk, Siberia
-40°C (-40°F) in northern Canada
-110°C (-166°F) on the Antarctic plateau
context: Vostok Station, a Russian research base in the interior of Antarctica, recorded -89.2°C in July 1983 — still the coldest temperature ever measured at Earth's surface. The station sits at 3,488 metres altitude, which alone makes it extremely cold. Beneath it, under 4 km of ice, Lake Vostok is one of the largest lakes in the world — kept liquid by geothermal heat and insulated by the ice above. It has been isolated for perhaps 15–25 million years.

question: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area. Why is it called a 'sea' if it is actually a lake?
answer: It was once connected to ancient oceans and has saltwater
choices: It was once connected to ancient oceans and has saltwater
It is so large that it creates its own weather patterns like a real sea
International law requires bodies of water above a certain size to be called seas
Early explorers who named it mistakenly believed it connected to the ocean
context: The Caspian was part of the ancient Tethys Sea, which once connected it to the Mediterranean and Black Sea. When geological activity cut off the connection about 5 million years ago, it became landlocked but retained its salty water. Its salinity is about a third of the ocean. Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan all border it, and for decades they argued over whether it was a 'sea' (in which case each country owns a portion of the seabed) or a 'lake' (shared resource). The distinction matters because the Caspian seabed contains enormous oil and gas reserves.

question: The Red Sea separates Africa from the Arabian Peninsula. What is slowly happening to it?
answer: It is slowly widening as the two plates pull apart
choices: It is slowly widening as the two plates pull apart
It is slowly getting shallower as sediment fills it from both sides
It is slowly shrinking as evaporation exceeds water flowing in
It is slowly getting saltier as rivers stop flowing into it
context: The Red Sea sits in a rift valley — a crack where the African and Arabian tectonic plates are pulling apart at about 1–2 cm per year. In geological terms, the Red Sea is a young ocean in its earliest stage. The East African Rift Valley is an earlier stage of the same process. In tens of millions of years, East Africa will separate from the rest of the continent, and the Red Sea will widen into a full-sized ocean — just as the Atlantic once started as a narrow rift between Africa and the Americas.

question: Fjords are long, narrow inlets found along the coasts of Norway, New Zealand, Chile, and Alaska. What carved them?
answer: Glaciers
choices: Glaciers
Rivers
Earthquakes splitting the coastline
Rising sea levels flooding mountain valleys
context: During ice ages, massive glaciers flow down mountain valleys toward the sea, grinding out deep U-shaped troughs as they go. When the ice age ends and the glaciers retreat, the sea floods these deep valleys, creating fjords. Norway's Sognefjord is the world's deepest fjord — 1,308 metres deep and 204 km long. Fjords are famously scenic because the glacier carved the valley walls nearly vertical, so mountains plunge directly into the water. The word 'fjord' comes from Old Norse.

question: The International Date Line runs through the Pacific Ocean. What happens when a ship or plane crosses it heading west?
answer: You skip forward one day on the calendar
choices: You skip forward one day on the calendar
You go back one day on the calendar
You enter the next time zone but the date stays the same
Nothing — the date line only affects clocks, not calendars
context: Earth is divided into 24 time zones, and when you've crossed all of them going west, you've gained a full day in local time — so you must skip a calendar day to reset. Going east, you'd repeat a day. The Date Line roughly follows the 180° meridian but zigzags to avoid splitting countries and island groups. When Samoa shifted from one side of the Date Line to the other in 2011, residents went to sleep on Thursday 29 December and woke up on Saturday 31 December — skipping Friday entirely.

question: The Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest human-made structure in the world for almost 4,000 years. What eventually surpassed it?
answer: Lincoln Cathedral in England, completed around 1311 AD
choices: Lincoln Cathedral in England, completed around 1311 AD
The Eiffel Tower in Paris, completed in 1889
The Colosseum in Rome, completed around 80 AD
The Hagia Sophia in Istanbul, completed in 537 AD
context: The Great Pyramid stood 146.5 metres tall when completed around 2560 BCE. For 3,800 years, nothing human-made surpassed it. Lincoln Cathedral's central spire (now gone) reached about 160 metres when completed in 1311. The Eiffel Tower then demolished both records in 1889 at 300 metres. The Great Pyramid was built by the Egyptian pharaoh Khufu using about 2.3 million stone blocks averaging 2.5 tonnes each. How they were moved and placed with such precision remains a subject of genuine debate.

question: The Ural Mountains in Russia are important not just geographically but symbolically. What line do they mark?
answer: The boundary between Europe and Asia
choices: The boundary between Europe and Asia
The boundary between Russia's western and eastern time zones
The edge of the Siberian permafrost zone
The divide between Russia's two largest river drainage systems
context: The Urals are relatively low mountains — their highest peak is just 1,895 metres — but they've served as the conventional border between Europe and Asia since 18th-century geographers designated them so. Russia spans both continents: European Russia (west of the Urals) has most of the population and major cities, while Asian Russia (Siberia, east of the Urals) is vast, cold, and sparsely populated but rich in oil, gas, diamonds, and timber. The 'line' is really more of a cultural and historical convention than a strict geographic border.

question: Ocean water is salty, but rivers and lakes that flow into the ocean are usually freshwater. Where does the ocean's salt come from?
answer: Rivers wash dissolved minerals from rocks into the sea, where water evaporates but salt stays
choices: Rivers wash dissolved minerals from rocks into the sea, where water evaporates but salt stays
Underwater volcanoes release salt directly into the water
The seafloor is made of salt deposits that slowly dissolve upward
Salt falls from the atmosphere in rain and accumulates over billions of years
context: Rain is slightly acidic and slowly dissolves minerals — including sodium and chloride — from rocks as it flows downhill. Rivers carry these dissolved minerals to the sea. The sun evaporates the water back into clouds, but the salt stays behind. Over billions of years, the salt concentration in the ocean has been slowly rising. The ocean is currently about 3.5% salt on average. Some landlocked seas, like the Dead Sea and the Great Salt Lake, are far saltier because evaporation is even higher relative to inflow.

question: The Swiss Alps receive enormous amounts of snow every winter. Where does the meltwater from those mountains end up?
answer: In rivers flowing to the North Sea, Mediterranean, and Black Sea
choices: In rivers flowing to the North Sea, Mediterranean, and Black Sea
Mostly in glaciers that store it for thousands of years
In the Rhine River which carries all Swiss water to the Atlantic
In underground aquifers beneath the Alps
context: Switzerland sits on Europe's central watershed — rainwater and snowmelt flows north via the Rhine to the North Sea, south via the Po to the Adriatic and Mediterranean, and east via the Danube to the Black Sea. This is why Switzerland, despite having no seacoast, plays an outsized role in European water supply. The Rhine, Rhône, Inn (a Danube tributary), and Ticino all originate in the Swiss Alps. The Swiss call their country 'the water tower of Europe.'

question: The Amazon River has no bridges crossing it for most of its length. Why?
answer: The river floods so widely that a fixed bridge would need to be impractically long
choices: The river floods so widely that a fixed bridge would need to be impractically long
The rainforest on both banks is so dense that no roads connect to the river
The riverbed is too soft to support bridge foundations
Brazil's government has always preferred ferry crossings to bridges
context: During wet season, the Amazon can be 50 km wide in places, and the surrounding rainforest floods to an additional 20–100 km on each side. A bridge would need to span not just the main channel but this entire flood plain — an engineering challenge of extraordinary scale. There are no roads linking northern and southern Brazil across the Amazon basin. The first fixed link across the river, the Manaus–Iranduba Bridge, only opened in 2011, and it took 40 years of planning.

question: What is the world's second-largest rainforest after the Amazon?
answer: The Congo Basin rainforest in Central Africa
choices: The Congo Basin rainforest in Central Africa
The Daintree Rainforest in Australia
The Borneo rainforest in Southeast Asia
The Valdivian rainforest in Chile
context: The Congo Basin covers about 3.7 million square kilometres — roughly the size of Western Europe — and is home to forest elephants, bonobos, okapis, and gorillas. Like the Amazon, it produces enormous amounts of oxygen and stores vast quantities of carbon. The Congo River, which drains this basin, carries the most water of any river in the world after the Amazon. Unlike the Amazon, which is increasingly threatened by agriculture, large parts of the Congo Basin remain intact — mainly because the region is relatively inaccessible.

question: The Great Pyramid at Giza has almost perfectly level foundations despite covering 5.3 hectares of desert. How accurate is it?
answer: The base is level to within 2.1 centimetres across its entire area
choices: The base is level to within 2.1 centimetres across its entire area
The base is level to within about 1 metre across its area
The base was perfectly level when built but has since settled unevenly
The pyramid's base is not level — it tilts about 1 degree toward the Nile
context: The ancient Egyptians likely used water-filled trenches to establish a level baseline — water always finds its own level. They then cut the bedrock and filled the trenches to achieve precision that modern surveys confirm is within 2.1 cm across the entire 230-metre base. The four sides face the cardinal directions with an error of less than 0.05 degrees. How 2.3 million blocks averaging 2.5 tonnes each were quarried, transported, and placed with this accuracy without modern tools remains one of history's great engineering mysteries.

question: Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania is Africa's highest peak. What is extraordinary about where the snow on its summit comes from?
answer: It sits almost exactly on the equator, so its snow comes despite year-round tropical sun
choices: It sits almost exactly on the equator, so its snow comes despite year-round tropical sun
Ocean storms from the Indian Ocean dump snow directly on its upper slopes
Underground water freezes as it rises through the volcanic rock and emerges as ice
The mountain's height pulls moisture from passing jet streams
context: Kilimanjaro's summit at 5,895 metres is cold enough to maintain glaciers and snow even though it sits at 3°S latitude — just 330 km from the equator. But those glaciers are retreating rapidly. Since 1912, Kilimanjaro has lost over 80% of its ice cap. At current rates, it could be ice-free by 2040. The mountain is actually a dormant volcano; its caldera at the summit is called Kibo. About 50,000 people attempt to climb it each year.

question: The Philippine Trench in the Pacific is one of the deepest parts of the ocean. The Philippines also experiences some of the world's most destructive typhoons. What connects both facts?
answer: The Philippines sits on the edge of tectonic plates where cold ocean floor dives deep, while the warm surrounding ocean fuels typhoons
choices: The Philippines sits on the edge of tectonic plates where cold ocean floor dives deep, while the warm surrounding ocean fuels typhoons
The trench funnels cold water upward which collides with warm air to create storms
Both are caused by the same jet stream that circles the Pacific
The volcanic islands created by the trench warm the nearby water to typhoon-generating temperatures
context: The Philippines sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire: the Pacific Plate dives beneath the Philippine Sea Plate, creating the Philippine Trench (10,540 m deep) and fuelling the country's volcanoes. The warm shallow seas surrounding the archipelago also provide the heat energy that powers typhoons. The Philippines experiences about 20 typhoons per year — more than almost any other country. The deadliest was Typhoon Haiyan in 2013, which killed over 6,300 people with winds of 315 km/h.

question: The Nile is often called the world's longest river, but some measurements give the title to the Amazon. Why is there disagreement?
answer: River length depends on where you define the source and how you measure the twists
choices: River length depends on where you define the source and how you measure the twists
The two rivers are exactly the same length and scientists cannot decide which is longer
The Amazon is longer but the Nile is broader, so different criteria give different winners
The measurements were done in different centuries and cannot be accurately compared
context: The 'source' of a river is the furthest point from the mouth that still flows toward it — but rivers have many tributaries, and which one you choose as the 'main' branch changes the answer. The Nile is traditionally measured at 6,650 km; the Amazon at 6,400 km. But Brazilian scientists measured the Amazon at 6,992 km using a different source point. Neither the Nile nor Amazon starts from a spring — both begin in distant highland lakes whose exact boundaries are debatable.

question: The Colosseum in Rome was built nearly 2,000 years ago and still stands, yet much of its original stone is missing. Where did it go?
answer: It was quarried and reused to build other buildings in Rome, including parts of St. Peter's Basilica
choices: It was quarried and reused to build other buildings in Rome, including parts of St. Peter's Basilica
The stone dissolved slowly over centuries because of acidic rainwater
Earthquakes cracked and toppled large sections which were never rebuilt
It was dismantled by medieval rulers who saw it as a pagan monument
context: For centuries, the Colosseum was treated as a convenient quarry. Popes and Roman nobles systematically stripped its travertine marble, bronze clamps, and stone to use in new buildings. The estimated 100,000 cubic metres of stone removed could build a small city. Pope Sixtus V even planned to convert it into a wool factory before his death halted the plan. What saved the rest was a papal declaration in 1749 that it was sacred ground, hallowed by the blood of Christian martyrs.

question: Pangaea was the name given to the ancient supercontinent. How long ago did it start breaking apart?
answer: About 175 million years ago
choices: About 175 million years ago
About 10,000 years ago
About 65 million years ago
About 1 billion years ago
context: Pangaea began breaking apart in the Jurassic period — when dinosaurs roamed the Earth. It first split into Laurasia (north) and Gondwana (south), which then fragmented further. The Atlantic Ocean didn't exist when Pangaea was intact; it formed as North America pulled away from Europe. India was once part of Gondwana; it drifted north and crashed into Asia about 50 million years ago, creating the Himalayas. This collision is still happening — the Himalayas grow a few millimetres taller every year.

question: The Amazon is the world's largest river by water volume. How much of Earth's total river water does it carry?
answer: About one fifth of all river water on Earth
choices: About one fifth of all river water on Earth
About half of all river water on Earth
About one tenth of all river water on Earth
About three quarters of all river water on Earth
context: The Amazon discharges around 209,000 cubic metres of water into the Atlantic every second at peak flow — about 20% of all freshwater discharged by rivers worldwide. By comparison, the Congo, the second-largest by discharge, carries about 4%. The Amazon's enormous flow comes from the combination of heavy rainfall across the world's largest rainforest and drainage from the vast Andes mountains to the west. The basin covers about 7 million square kilometres — almost the size of Australia.

question: The Arctic region is warming faster than anywhere else on Earth. What is disappearing there that scientists are most worried about?
answer: Sea ice
choices: Sea ice
Polar bears
Permafrost tundra plants
The ozone layer above the pole
context: Arctic sea ice reflects about 80% of sunlight back into space. As it melts, it exposes dark ocean water that absorbs 94% of sunlight, heating the water further and melting more ice — a feedback loop that makes the Arctic warm about four times faster than the global average. Since 1979, the Arctic has lost about 13% of its sea ice per decade. Some scientists predict an ice-free Arctic summer by the 2040s. This affects not just polar bears but global weather patterns, ocean currents, and sea levels.

question: The Sahara Desert covers most of northern Africa. Which two geographic features to its south and north keep it a desert?
answer: The Atlas Mountains to the north and the tropical rainforest belt to the south
choices: The Atlas Mountains to the north and the tropical rainforest belt to the south
The Mediterranean Sea to the north and the Congo River to the south
The Atlantic Ocean to the west and the Red Sea to the east
The Nile River to the east and the Sahel grasslands to the south
context: The Sahara is held in place by two atmospheric phenomena. To the north, sinking air from the subtropical high-pressure belt (the same system that creates the world's other major deserts at 30°N and 30°S) suppresses rainfall. To the south, the intertropical convergence zone brings the monsoon rains that feed the Sahel. The Sahara's exact borders have shifted dramatically over geological time — it was green and wet as recently as 5,000 years ago — and small shifts in Earth's orbit can flip it between desert and savanna.

question: Iceland and Greenland have somewhat misleading names. Which one actually has more green, habitable land?
answer: Iceland
choices: Iceland
Greenland
Both have similar amounts of habitable land
Neither — both are mostly ice and rock
context: The story goes that Erik the Red, banished from Iceland in 982 AD, discovered Greenland and gave it an attractive name to lure settlers. Iceland, meanwhile, does have some green, farming land — about 20% of its surface — especially near the coasts. Greenland is 80% covered by its ice sheet; only a narrow coastal strip is ice-free. Iceland has about 370,000 people; Greenland has about 56,000. The naming is almost perfectly backwards, though Iceland does have a lot of lava fields and Greenland's ice-free coast does have some green meadows.

question: The city of Amsterdam in the Netherlands is famously built on canals. Why was Amsterdam originally built in such a wet location?
answer: The marshy ground at the mouth of the Amstel River offered protection from attack and access to trade
choices: The marshy ground at the mouth of the Amstel River offered protection from attack and access to trade
The Dutch were expert sailors who preferred living close to water above all else
The city was built on a natural island that happened to be in a river delta
The site was chosen because underground springs provided clean freshwater
context: Amsterdam was founded around 1275 where the Amstel River met the IJ bay. The soggy, marshy ground was difficult to attack overland and provided easy access to sea trade. The Dutch drained the land, drove wooden piles into the mud, and built on top. Amsterdam sits on about 11 million wooden piles. The city grew rich trading Baltic grain, herring, spices from Asia, and later goods from across the globe, becoming one of the most important cities in the world in the 17th century.

question: Africa is the world's second-largest continent. In what surprising way is it larger than most maps suggest?
answer: Common map projections make it look much smaller than it actually is
choices: Common map projections make it look much smaller than it actually is
Newly discovered islands in African waters have recently expanded its official boundaries
Sea level fall has exposed large continental shelf areas around its coasts
Africa's borders were redrawn after independence to include offshore territories
context: The Mercator projection — the most commonly used world map — inflates areas far from the equator. Greenland appears the same size as Africa on a Mercator map, but Africa is actually 14 times larger. The USA, China, India, Europe, and Japan could all fit inside Africa simultaneously with room to spare. Africa covers 30.3 million square kilometres. It looks small partly because it straddles the equator, where Mercator causes the least distortion — so Africa is ironically shown most accurately while everything else is inflated.

question: Japan experiences thousands of earthquakes each year. What geographic reason makes Japan so earthquake-prone?
answer: It sits where four tectonic plates meet
choices: It sits where four tectonic plates meet
It is built on unusually thin ocean crust that vibrates easily
Its many volcanoes cause the ground to shake with each eruption
The heavy snow in winter compresses the ground and causes it to spring back
context: Japan sits at the junction of the Pacific, Philippine, Eurasian, and North American plates — one of the most tectonically active spots on Earth. The Pacific Plate dives under Japan at the Japan Trench, generating large earthquakes and the volcanic arc that created the Japanese islands. Japan experiences about 1,500 earthquakes per year strong enough to be felt, and has had several devastating megaquakes including the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake (magnitude 9.0) which triggered the Fukushima nuclear disaster.

question: The Sahara is the world's largest hot desert. Yet the Sahara is not the world's most remote desert — some parts can be reached easily. What percent of the Sahara is actually covered by sand dunes?
answer: About 25%
choices: About 25%
About 90%
About 5%
About 60%
context: The popular image of a desert as endless rolling sand dunes (ergs) accounts for only about a quarter of the Sahara. Most of it is rocky plateau (hammada), gravel plains (reg), and mountain ranges. The Tibesti Mountains in Chad rise to over 3,000 metres within the Sahara. Sand dunes form where wind concentrates loose sand, which requires specific conditions. The Sahara's most spectacular erg is the Erg Chebbi in Morocco, whose photogenic orange dunes attract tourists — but they represent the exception, not the rule.

question: The Mekong River flows through six countries on its way to the sea. Which country does it empty into?
answer: Vietnam
choices: Vietnam
Thailand
Cambodia
China
context: The Mekong rises on the Tibetan Plateau in China and flows 4,350 km through Yunnan province, Myanmar, Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, and finally empties into the South China Sea through the Mekong Delta in Vietnam. The river sustains one of the world's largest freshwater fisheries — about 60 million people depend on it for food. Massive Chinese dams upstream have dramatically reduced downstream flow and fish populations, causing serious tensions between China and the lower Mekong nations.

question: The Tropic of Cancer and Tropic of Capricorn mark important lines of latitude. What happens at those lines?
answer: The sun is directly overhead at noon at least once a year
choices: The sun is directly overhead at noon at least once a year
The seasons change from summer to autumn and from spring to summer
Day and night are exactly equal in length throughout the year
The magnetic poles align with the geographic poles at these latitudes
context: The Tropic of Cancer is at 23.5°N; the Tropic of Capricorn is at 23.5°S. These mark how far north and south the sun can be directly overhead — a consequence of Earth's axial tilt. The region between the two tropics (the tropics) receives the most intense sunlight on Earth and has no real winter. On the June solstice, the sun is directly overhead at the Tropic of Cancer; on the December solstice, at the Tropic of Capricorn. The word 'tropic' comes from the Greek for 'turning' — these are the latitudes where the sun appears to turn back.

question: Lake Titicaca on the border of Bolivia and Peru is the world's highest navigable lake. How high above sea level is it?
answer: About 3,810 metres — higher than most Alpine peaks
choices: About 3,810 metres — higher than most Alpine peaks
About 1,200 metres — about the height of a tall hill
About 6,000 metres — near the altitude of Everest base camp
About 500 metres — roughly the height of a tall building
context: Titicaca sits on the Altiplano — a high plateau between two ranges of the Andes. The altitude is so extreme that visitors from sea level commonly experience altitude sickness. Locals, including the Uros people who live on floating reed islands on the lake, are adapted to low oxygen: they have larger lungs and higher red blood cell counts than sea-level populations. The lake moderates temperatures on the surrounding plateau — its enormous volume of water warms slowly and cools slowly, preventing the brutal frosts that would otherwise destroy crops.

question: The Himalayas are the world's highest mountain range. What two landmasses collided to create them?
answer: The Indian subcontinent and Asia
choices: The Indian subcontinent and Asia
Africa and Europe
Australia and Southeast Asia
Antarctica and South America
context: India was once part of the southern supercontinent Gondwana. About 50–70 million years ago, it broke free and drifted north at unusually high speed (for plate tectonics) — about 15–20 cm per year. When it crashed into Asia, there was no subduction — both were continental crust, too buoyant to sink. Instead, the crust crumpled and piled upward, creating the Himalayas and the Tibetan Plateau. India is still moving north at about 5 cm per year, so the Himalayas are still growing a few millimetres annually.

question: Which ocean is the youngest — meaning it has existed for the shortest amount of time?
answer: The Atlantic Ocean
choices: The Atlantic Ocean
The Indian Ocean
The Arctic Ocean
The Pacific Ocean
context: The Atlantic began forming about 180 million years ago when the supercontinent Pangaea started rifting apart and North America began separating from Europe and Africa. The Pacific, by contrast, is a remnant of the ancient Panthalassa Ocean that surrounded Pangaea and is over 700 million years old. The Atlantic is still widening — the Mid-Atlantic Ridge is an active spreading zone — while the Pacific is actually shrinking as ocean crust dives under the surrounding continents.

question: Coral reefs support about 25% of all marine species but cover less than 1% of the ocean floor. What do corals need to survive?
answer: Warm, clear, shallow, sunlit water
choices: Warm, clear, shallow, sunlit water
Cold, deep, nutrient-rich water near polar currents
Freshwater mixing zones where rivers meet the sea
Any ocean water with sufficient salt concentration
context: Reef-building corals need sunlight for the algae (zooxanthellae) that live inside them and provide up to 90% of their energy. This limits reefs to depths of about 60 metres or less. They also need warm water (above 18°C, ideally 23–29°C) and cannot tolerate fresh water. When water gets too warm, corals expel their algae and turn white — a process called bleaching. Without their algae, they starve. The Great Barrier Reef experienced mass bleaching events in 2016, 2017, 2020, and 2022.

question: The Drake Passage between South America and Antarctica is one of the world's most dangerous stretches of ocean. Why?
answer: Winds can travel around the entire Southern Ocean without hitting any land, building enormous waves
choices: Winds can travel around the entire Southern Ocean without hitting any land, building enormous waves
Icebergs from Antarctica frequently drift into the shipping lane and sink ships
The magnetic south pole is nearby, causing navigation instruments to malfunction
The water is so cold that ships' hulls become brittle and crack
context: The Drake Passage is the only place on Earth where ocean and wind can circle the globe completely unobstructed by land. This generates the Roaring Forties, Furious Fifties, and Screaming Sixties — latitude bands named for the ferocious westerly winds. Wave heights of 15+ metres are common; sailors have reported 30-metre waves. Before the Panama Canal opened in 1914, Cape Horn at the tip of South America was the only route between the Atlantic and Pacific. It sank hundreds of ships and earned the nickname 'the Cape of Storms.'

question: Mangrove forests grow at the edge of tropical coastlines where rivers meet the sea. What makes them unusual as trees?
answer: Their roots grow above the water and can survive in saltwater
choices: Their roots grow above the water and can survive in saltwater
They shed their leaves during the wet season instead of the dry season
Their wood is hollow and fills with air to keep the tree from sinking
They reproduce by releasing seeds that swim to new locations like fish
context: Mangroves are among the few trees that can survive with their roots constantly submerged in salty, oxygen-poor water. Their complex root systems trap sediment and protect coastlines from storm surge and erosion. They are also crucial nurseries — about 80% of commercial fish species worldwide spend part of their lives in mangroves. Despite covering less than 0.5% of Earth's land surface, they store carbon up to 5 times more efficiently than tropical rainforests. About 35% of the world's mangroves have been destroyed, mainly for shrimp farming.

question: The city of São Paulo in Brazil has a population of over 20 million people. Why did this city become so enormous?
answer: It grew into a coffee trade hub and then a manufacturing and industrial centre
choices: It grew into a coffee trade hub and then a manufacturing and industrial centre
It was chosen as Brazil's capital and government workers flooded in over centuries
It sits at the mouth of the Amazon and became a river trade hub
Portuguese colonisers founded it as a fortified port to defend the coast
context: São Paulo started as a Jesuit mission in 1554. It grew modestly until the 19th-century coffee boom turned the surrounding region into the world's largest coffee producer. Railways connecting coffee plantations to São Paulo made it a natural processing and trading centre. Immigrants poured in from Italy, Japan, Syria, and across the world to work in coffee and later manufacturing. Today São Paulo has the largest Japanese population outside Japan, and its skyline rivals New York's. It sits 70 km from the sea at 760 metres altitude.

question: The South Pole is in Antarctica, and the North Pole is in the Arctic. What is the key difference between the two poles?
answer: The South Pole is on land; the North Pole is floating on ocean water covered in sea ice
choices: The South Pole is on land; the North Pole is floating on ocean water covered in sea ice
The South Pole is colder because it is higher above sea level
The North Pole has permanent ice year-round while the South Pole is ice-free in summer
The South Pole has the midnight sun in December while the North Pole has it in June
context: The South Pole sits on Antarctica — a continent with a thick ice sheet. The US Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station is a permanent research base there. The North Pole, by contrast, is in the middle of the Arctic Ocean, covered by sea ice that shifts with the currents. There is no land at the North Pole. A submarine can reach the North Pole by travelling underwater; reaching the South Pole requires crossing the Antarctic continent. The Arctic is warming so fast that within decades it may be ice-free in summer.

question: The Andes Mountains are the longest mountain chain in the world. Along which coast of South America do they run?
answer: The western (Pacific) coast
choices: The western (Pacific) coast
The eastern (Atlantic) coast
The northern coast facing the Caribbean
Through the interior of the continent
context: The Andes stretch 7,000 km along South America's Pacific coast from Venezuela to Tierra del Fuego. They formed because the Nazca Plate (Pacific Ocean floor) is diving beneath the South American Plate — a process called subduction. This creates volcanoes, earthquakes, and the crumpling that built the mountains. The Andes are young by geological standards (mostly formed in the last 25 million years) and still rising. They act as a massive barrier: the Pacific side is the Atacama Desert; the eastern slopes receive Amazon rainfall and feed the continent's great rivers.

question: Rivers that empty into the sea carry the sediment they've eroded. What landform do they often build at their mouths?
answer: A delta
choices: A delta
A fjord
An estuary
A lagoon
context: When a river slows down as it enters the sea, it drops the sediment it's been carrying. Over time, this sediment builds up into flat, fan-shaped land that pushes the river mouth outward — a delta. The Nile Delta in Egypt was so fertile it sustained one of history's greatest civilisations for 3,000 years. The word 'delta' comes from the Greek letter Δ (triangle), which matches the Nile Delta's shape. Today the Nile Delta is shrinking because dams upstream trap the sediment before it reaches the sea.

question: Papua New Guinea is one of the most linguistically diverse places on Earth. How many languages are spoken there?
answer: About 840 languages — more than any other country
choices: About 840 languages — more than any other country
About 50 languages — one per major island group
About 200 languages — one per major tribal territory
About 12 languages — one per province
context: Papua New Guinea has about 840 known living languages — roughly 12% of all human languages — in a country of about 10 million people. This extreme linguistic diversity developed over tens of thousands of years because the rugged mountain terrain separated communities from each other so effectively that they evolved distinct languages. Many villages were so isolated that the next valley was effectively a foreign country. The country uses Tok Pisin (a creole) as a national lingua franca because no single local language dominates.

question: The Sahel is a semi-arid strip of land stretching across Africa just south of the Sahara. What does the Arabic word 'Sahel' mean?
answer: Shore or coast
choices: Shore or coast
Desert edge
Dry grassland
Meeting place
context: Arab geographers called the Sahel the 'shore' of the Sahara — the place where the desert meets the vegetation. The Sahel stretches from Senegal to Ethiopia, about 5,400 km, and 200–400 km wide. It's a fragile transition zone: slightly more rain means grassland; slightly less means desert. In the 1970s–80s, the Sahara expanded southward through the Sahel due to drought and overgrazing — a process called desertification. More recently, ambitious tree-planting projects are trying to create a 'Great Green Wall' across the continent.

question: The city of Machu Picchu in Peru sits at about 2,430 metres altitude in the Andes. What civilisation built it?
answer: The Inca
choices: The Inca
The Aztec
The Maya
The Tiwanaku
context: Machu Picchu was built around 1450 AD as an estate for the Inca emperor Pachacuti. It was abandoned about a century later, possibly due to a smallpox epidemic, and remained unknown to the outside world until American historian Hiram Bingham brought it to international attention in 1911. It was never hidden from local people — farmers worked the terraces for centuries — but Spanish colonisers apparently never found or destroyed it. The Inca built it without iron tools, wheels, or draft animals, fitting stones together so precisely that no mortar was needed.

question: The island of Borneo is shared between three countries. Which three?
answer: Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei
choices: Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei
Indonesia, Philippines, and Malaysia
Thailand, Indonesia, and Malaysia
Philippines, Malaysia, and Brunei
context: Borneo is the world's third-largest island. Malaysia's states of Sabah and Sarawak occupy the north; tiny Brunei is a sovereign nation on the northwest coast (famous for its enormous oil wealth); the rest — the vast majority — is the Indonesian region of Kalimantan. The island's rainforests are among the world's most biodiverse, home to orangutans, pygmy elephants, and proboscis monkeys. Those forests have been dramatically reduced by logging and conversion to palm oil plantations over the past 50 years.

question: Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD and buried Pompeii. Is Vesuvius still an active volcano today?
answer: Yes — it is considered one of the most dangerous volcanoes in the world
choices: Yes — it is considered one of the most dangerous volcanoes in the world
No — it became dormant after the 79 AD eruption and has been quiet since
No — a 1631 eruption was the last significant one and volcanologists consider it extinct
Yes — but only in the sense that it still releases gases, not lava or ash
context: Vesuvius last erupted in 1944, damaging villages and destroying Allied aircraft parked nearby. About 3 million people live in the 'red zone' around Vesuvius — the area that would need to be evacuated if it shows signs of erupting. Volcanologists consider it one of the world's most dangerous volcanoes partly because of its proximity to Naples (3 million people) and partly because it is due for a large 'Plinian' eruption similar to 79 AD. Italy has detailed evacuation plans, but has never done a full-scale drill.

question: The Nile River was ancient Egypt's lifeblood. What annual event made Egyptian agriculture possible in a desert?
answer: The Nile flooded every summer, depositing rich black silt across the fields
choices: The Nile flooded every summer, depositing rich black silt across the fields
Underground springs kept the soil moist year-round beneath the desert surface
The Mediterranean Sea pushed freshwater up the Nile each spring
Seasonal rains from the Red Sea blew inland and watered the Nile Valley
context: Each summer, monsoon rains in the Ethiopian highlands sent a surge of water and nutrient-rich sediment down the Nile. When the floodwaters retreated in September, they left behind a layer of black silt so fertile that Egyptians called their land Kemet — 'the black land.' Crops grew in the floodplain while the surrounding desert remained barren. The Aswan High Dam, completed in 1970, ended the floods permanently — protecting Egypt from drought but also stopping the delivery of sediment that had sustained its agriculture for 7,000 years.

question: Which country has the most natural lakes in the world?
answer: Canada
choices: Canada
Russia
Finland
Brazil
context: Canada has over 2 million lakes covering about 9% of its land surface — more than any other country. This is largely a legacy of the last ice age: glaciers carved thousands of basins in the Canadian Shield, and when the ice melted, they filled with water. Many of these lakes have no outlet and exist purely because the ice-scoured rock holds water. Canada contains about 20% of the world's total freshwater. The Great Lakes, shared with the USA, hold about 21% of the world's surface freshwater on their own.

question: The Danube River flows through more countries than any other river in the world. How many countries does it pass through?
answer: Ten countries
choices: Ten countries
Four countries
Seven countries
Fifteen countries
context: The Danube rises in Germany's Black Forest and flows 2,860 km through Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Bulgaria, Romania, Moldova, and Ukraine before emptying into the Black Sea. It has served as a trade artery, military highway, and political boundary for 2,000 years. The Romans used it as the northern border of their empire — the fortresses they built along it grew into modern cities including Vienna and Budapest. Waltz composer Johann Strauss II immortalised it in 'The Blue Danube,' though the river is actually brown with sediment.

question: The world's largest living structure is a reef system visible from space. But all coral reefs together cover less than what fraction of the ocean floor?
answer: Less than one percent
choices: Less than one percent
About ten percent
About five percent
About twenty-five percent
context: Coral reefs cover about 284,000 square kilometres — less than 0.1% of the ocean floor — yet they shelter an estimated 25% of all marine species. Scientists sometimes call them the rainforests of the sea. Roughly half a billion people depend on coral reefs for food and income. They also protect coastlines: a healthy reef absorbs up to 97% of a wave's energy before it reaches the shore.
